task_yaml.task:
I'm gathering historical images I can actually reuse, from an archive's
catalog. Help me pick 5 records that each have a genuinely usable, openly-
licensed full image.

A record is only usable if ALL of these hold:
- the ITEM-level rights are clearly open ("No known restrictions" or "Public
  domain"). Watch out: the collection-level rights can look open while the
  specific item is actually restricted — the item-level rights are what
  matter.
- a full / high-resolution image is actually available, not just a thumbnail.
- the item really is a visual image (a photograph), not an OCR'd text
  manuscript or a sound recording that happens to have a scan.
- duplicates are collapsed: some records are the SAME image catalogued twice
  (they share a `dedup_group`) — count such an image only once.

The catalog is at:

    /tmp_workspace/clawbench/sources/records/

Each record `<id>.json` has `type`, `collection_rights`, `item_rights`, an
`image` block (`thumbnail`, `full_image`, `iiif`, `width`), and a
`dedup_group`. `index.json` restates the rules.

Go through every record, apply the four rules, and select 5 distinct usable
images.

Save:
- `/tmp_workspace/results/usable_images.json`: the 5 selected records, each
  with id, title, the item-level rights evidence, the full-image evidence,
  the type, and the dedup_group it represents.
- `/tmp_workspace/results/rejected_records.json`: every rejected/duplicate
  record with the SPECIFIC reason (thumbnail-only, item-level restricted,
  not-an-image, rights-undetermined, or duplicate-of-<id>), citing the field.
- `/tmp_workspace/results/selection_method.json`: how you applied the
  item-vs-collection rights rule and the dedup rule (reproducible).

This is fully offline — do not fetch anything from the network.
